description: New government digital service, IT system replacement, or major technology upgrade. Covers citizen-facing services, back-office systems, and data platforms.

small_digital_service.note: Single citizen-facing service (e.g., online application form)

large_platform.note: Cross-government platform (e.g., Universal Credit IT). Reference: NAO Major Projects data.

ongoing_cost_pct_of_build.note: Annual running costs typically 15-30% of initial build cost for government IT

typical_benefits.description: Automation of manual processes, reduced processing time
typical_benefits.monetisation: Staff time saved x average salary. Be conservative: automation rarely eliminates roles entirely (usually reallocated).

typical_benefits.description: Fewer manual errors in data entry, processing, payments
typical_benefits.monetisation: Cost of correcting errors x volume x error rate reduction

typical_benefits.description: Faster, simpler online services vs paper/phone
typical_benefits.monetisation: DfT TAG leisure time value (~GBP 6/hr) applied to citizen time saved. Caution: small per-transaction savings, large volumes.

typical_benefits.description: Better data, analytics, reporting enables better policy decisions
typical_benefits.monetisation: Very difficult to monetise. Present as non-monetised benefit with qualitative description.

typical_benefits.description: Improved data matching and verification reduces fraud/error
typical_benefits.monetisation: Expected fraud/error reduction x current fraud/error value. Reference: HMRC, DWP fraud and error statistics.

typical_benefits.description: Reduced running costs of old systems
typical_benefits.monetisation: Direct cost comparison: legacy running cost minus new system running cost

default_parameters.appraisal_period_note: IT systems have shorter economic lives than physical assets. 7-12 years typical before major refresh. Green Book supplementary guidance: IT systems 5-10 years.
default_parameters.optimism_bias_note: Equipment/development projects have the highest optimism bias in the Green Book table (up to 200% at SOC). IT projects are notorious for cost overruns.
default_parameters.additionality_note: Government IT projects are not subject to standard additionality adjustments.

reference_bcr_range.note: High variance. Small digital services can have very high BCRs (low cost, large user base). Large system replacements frequently have BCRs below 1.0 when full costs (including transition, training, data migration) are included.
reference_cases: Universal Credit IT (2013-ongoing): original BCR not published; NAO reported total cost GBP 1.3bn (vs original estimate GBP 0.4bn)
HMRC Making Tax Digital (2015-2024): BCR estimated at 1.3-2.0 depending on assumptions
GOV.UK single domain (2012): widely cited as high-BCR example (consolidated 300+ websites for ~GBP 10m)
NHS Spine 2 (2014): BCR reportedly >2.0 for the data-sharing platform
IPA Annual Report 2023: IT/digital projects have highest rate of 'red' RAG ratings of any category

typical_risks: Cost overruns: IT projects have the worst track record (Green Book OB: up to 200%)
Scope creep: requirements grow during development
Integration complexity: connecting to legacy systems, data migration
Organisational change: staff resistance, training requirements, process redesign
Vendor lock-in: proprietary technology constraining future options
Cybersecurity: new systems create new attack surfaces
Benefits realisation: efficiency savings often absorbed rather than realised (staff redeployed, not released)

publications: IPA Annual Report on Major Projects
Green Book Supplementary Guidance: Optimism Bias
